[141:43]they secured an agreement to do this summit,

[141:46]I think on Tuesday or Wednesday.

[141:49]We'll talk a little bit about the timeline here

[141:51]because it is very important to understand

[141:54]the course of the summit and the outcome.

[141:57]But this whole thing came together in a week.

[142:01]Steve Wittkoff, the special envoy, was deployed to Moscow,

[142:05]I think last Tuesday.

[142:10]that Trump had set previously for Putin to make a deal.

[142:17]did not come to the table and make a deal

[142:23]then there would be this significant escalation

[142:30]on Russian energy, which would most probably impact

[142:50]And so he shrugged off the ultimatum.

[142:55]I think Trump knew that.

[143:03]to agree to have this summit. And so Wittkoff was sent out just days before the deadline that Trump did not want to make good on.

[143:20]between Russia and the United States.

[146:58]This is the New York Times recap. It says, quote, President Trump and President Vladimir

[147:02]Putin of Russia concluded their summit in Alaska on Friday without declaring agreement

[147:07]on any issue. At a joint appearance after their nearly three hour meeting at joint base

[147:21]that progress had been made saying, quote,

[147:23]many points were agreed to and there are just a very few that are left.

[147:33]That's an important clarification. He said, we've made some headway,

[147:38]so there's no deal until there's a deal.

[148:37]to inform them of what had been said in the meeting.

[149:01]And I guess the most important thing to understand about the summit is why it took place at all.

[149:08]The background, the timeline of diplomacy between this new Trump administration and Russia.

[149:15]And I would characterize this entire thing as a charade.

[149:24]And the reason is very simple. This is what is important to understand about what is happening

[149:29]in Ukraine, about what is happening on the battlefield.

[149:33]Basically, Russia is winning. That's the problem. Somewhere in the last couple of years, 2023,

[149:42]2024, especially, and now in 2025, this war in Ukraine, it is about to enter its fourth

[149:52]in February, 2026, and it has become a war of attrition, meaning that this has become

[150:01]a war of resources, more than anything, because each side is matched technologically. And

[150:11]in terms of tactics and equipment, Ukraine has some defensive advantages. It has support

[150:20]And initially, in the early stages of the war, Ukraine was able to launch effective counter-attacks

[150:26]and was able to defend their front line.

[150:31]But both sides shifted around 23 and 24 into a full-blown war of attrition, meaning that

[150:38]they're not taking significant risks, the progress is very slow, and really it has turned

[150:49]And of course, Russia has the advantage in an attritional war.

[150:54]And that has to do with two important factors, their industrial base and their population.

[151:01]In a war of attrition, it tests who has the bigger base of manpower and who has more supplies.

[151:15]Of course, the people that are actually fighting the war are Ukrainians, even though they're

[151:19]using Western equipment and supplies and armor and intelligence and officers and tactics,

[151:26]and it's Western everything, but the actual soldiers are Ukrainian.

[151:31]And so in that way, Ukraine is at a significant disadvantage because its population is a fraction

[151:37]of the size of Russia's.

[151:39]So, in an attritional war, Russia has a major advantage in manpower.

[151:45]They can mobilize more soldiers, they can replace soldiers very quickly, and they've just got

[151:52]more of them.

[151:52]There's 650,000 Russian soldiers deployed on the frontline, easily replaceable.

[151:59]Russia can always mobilize more.

[152:02]Ukraine is rapidly running out of men.

[152:04]They just don't have the people.

[152:06]And that, by the way, is the biggest constraint

[152:10]on the Ukrainians.

[152:11]They just are not there.

[152:13]They just don't have the manpower.

[152:15]So many people have died in Ukraine,

[152:17]but specifically so many men have died

[152:20]that Ukraine is effectively demilitarized.

[152:24]One of Russia's objectives in the war is to demilitarize Ukraine.

[152:29]Ukraine has effectively been demilitarized

[152:31]because so many of the fighting age men have just simply died.

[152:36]And so they're running out of men to mobilize.

[152:39]The ones that are there are deserting.

[152:42]They just disappear.

[152:44]A significant percentage of them are just leaving the battlefield.

[152:48]They refuse to fight because morale is so low.

[152:51]And this becomes a death spiral.

[152:54]So the first factor in an attritional war, Russia by far and away has the advantage and

[152:59]that advantage is getting stronger every day, has been getting stronger every day and is

[153:03]starting to really become a problem.

[153:07]The second factor is equipment.

[153:09]And as I said, Ukraine is forced to rely on its own manpower for soldiers.

[153:16]But it can rely on the NATO alliance and the United States in particular for supplies,

[153:22]for missile interceptors, for drones, for tanks, for artillery, for the rest of it.

[153:28]But even there, Russia has the advantage.

[153:31]Russia has a much larger industrial base.

[153:35]More factories, more workshops, they make more artillery shells, more missiles, more

[153:41]Russia is making millions of drones every year. And the drone swarms are getting bigger

[153:46]all the time. Russia is able to mobilize over a thousand drones in a single swarm. And these

[153:55]are single use. These are suicide drones, first person view, kamikaze drones. And, you know,

[154:06]And Russia's able to make so many of them,

[154:08]like I said, millions per year,

[154:10]that they can, in one single strike,

[154:12]it will be cruise missiles.

[154:14]It will be big drones and small drones.

[154:18]And they're making so many of them

[154:20]that the swarms are getting bigger and bigger.

[154:22]In the early stages of the war,

[154:23]drones did not play a big part.

[154:25]Now they're a significant part

[154:27]of what's happening on the battlefield.

[154:28]And Russia just makes way more of them.

[154:31]way more of everything. So in both manpower and in equipment, Russia has the advantage.

[154:38]And what is happening on the battlefield is that this is becoming particularly acute.

[154:44]And specifically what is happening is this, Ukraine has created this defensive line,

[154:50]but as the war drags on, they do not have the manpower to control the entire perimeter.

[154:59]And so what is happening is that the Ukrainians are forced to in significant parts of the

[155:05]front line, they have to leave it defended only by landmines and drones.

[155:12]What that means is that significant parts of the front line do not have any soldiers.

[155:17]There are major gaps.

[155:19]They say it's like Swiss cheese.

[155:21]There's these huge gaps in the defensive line because they just, they do not have the manpower

[155:27]to fill the whole front line.

[155:29]So they're laying land mines, they're relying on drones.

[155:33]And what Russia is doing to exploit this is they're sending in very small dismounted teams

[155:47]They will secure the territory and then they will bring in the drones. They will bring in the armor. They will bring in the soldiers and

[155:56]So in some areas on the front line in the Donbas, they're getting around and encircling Ukrainian forces

[156:04]They're behind the Ukrainian line and

[156:07]what that means is that

[156:09]The Ukrainian line is at risk of complete collapse

[156:14]If you look at any of the military bloggers, anybody that's following the day to day updates,

[156:18]they are all saying the same thing.

[156:20]The frontline is Swiss cheese.

[156:22]It is undefended.

[156:24]They do not have enough people.

[156:26]Russia, through superior tactics, manpower and equipment is penetrating the frontline

[156:31]in circling, or the Ukrainian forces are at risk of encirclement.

[156:36]And it's not hard to see where this goes from here.

[156:39]And a matter of weeks or months, that line is just going to collapse.

[156:44]And when it does, the Russians are going to have a breakthrough.

[156:48]They are going to break through the front line and they are going to start to gain hundreds

[156:53]of kilometers of territory every day like they did in the early stages of the war.

[156:58]They are going to break out and they're going to race towards the Nieper River.

[157:05]The Ukrainian state is collapsing and the Ukrainian armed forces are collapsing.

[157:11]And as this goes on, these problems are compounded.

[157:16]As the Russians achieve more territorial gains, morale goes down.

[157:21]When morale goes down, the armed forces of Ukraine are deserting.

[157:26]It's more difficult to summon more manpower.

[157:29]And when desertion is high and manpower is in short supply, the Russians are able to

[157:35]push even harder.

[157:37]This is a death spiral.

[157:39]This is a very big problem.

[157:41]Now, what does this have to do with the diplomacy?

[157:44]So Russia is winning, suffice to say, as this has become an attritional war, as Ukraine's

[157:51]personnel problem becomes more acute, its manpower problem, as Russia makes more territorial

[157:57]advances and morale on the Ukrainian side drops, and all of this becomes a death spiral.

[158:03]What this means is that Russia does not really want to end the fighting.

[158:09]This is why in Washington, there has been a bit of a reality check in Europe and Ukraine. They are still delusional

[158:18]Zelensky and the Germans and the British and the French

[158:21]They all still believe that Russia is going to leave Ukrainian territory permanently

[158:26]That Ukraine is gonna get back all the territory. They've lost and Crimea and

[158:32]That's just delusional because who's gonna fight to take it back

[158:35]Ukraine has zero ability to counterattack, that's over.

[158:40]And they barely are able to defend their frontline.

[158:43]What they should do is a tactical retreat.

[158:46]So forget about taking territory back.

[158:48]It isn't happening anytime soon.

[158:51]In Washington, there's been a bit of a reality check, and this is why the American position

[158:59]Originally, the American position was the same maximalist position as the Europeans

[159:04]and Ukrainians.

[159:05]We said that we will never recognize Russia's territorial gains.

[159:10]We will never recognize them as Russian territory.

[159:13]We will never recognize that they're under Russian occupation.

[159:18]We're going to fight for every inch of Ukrainian territory back.

[159:22]With Trump in office, that has changed.

[159:27]And the two biggest ones are these, Trump said in February that NATO membership is off

[159:32]the table.

[159:34]We'll talk more about that in a moment.

[159:36]In addition, Trump has floated the idea that Ukraine is gonna have to give up some territory.

[159:42]He's waffled a bit on that, but he, unlike Biden, has floated the idea of land swaps,

[159:54]And the reason the United States is giving a little bit why they've come down from these

[159:59]maximal positions is because again, they recognize the reality on the battlefield.

[160:05]And the United States is saying, look, quit while you're ahead.

[160:09]If we can freeze the battle lines where they are and you lose the territory that is already

[160:14]under Russian occupation, consider yourself lucky because with each passing day, they're

[160:22]And so that's why the United States,

[160:24]our negotiating position is just freeze the battle lines,

[160:27]just stop the Russian advance.

[160:29]If we can get Russia to stop, then we could cut our losses.

[160:33]And there could be a comprehensive deal with Russia.

[160:37]Again, more on that later, that will include other issues.

[160:41]Like nuclear arms control, like the Arctic, like sanctions relief, we can make it an all-encompassing

[160:48]comprehensive deal.

[160:50]But Washington is saying, unlike Berlin, unlike London, unlike Paris, Washington is saying

[160:56]cut your losses.

[160:58]But Russia, understandably, they are going to keep pushing.

[161:02]They want to drag the war on as long as possible, because the longer the war goes on, the more

[161:10]The longer the war goes on, the more territory they get, and the longer the war goes on,

[161:16]they're going to get more territory faster.

[161:18]As the Ukrainian frontline collapses, the rate at which they expand is going to grow

[161:24]also.

[161:25]So Russia is really playing for time.

[161:27]If they can keep America and Ukraine and Europe divided, if they can keep the pressure

[161:38]And the more time they have, the more land they have.

[161:40]That is the strategy.

[161:44]Now, on the side of the United States, with regard to Trump, Trump has been very frustrated

[161:50]with Putin.

[161:52]Back in April, four months ago, Trump said to Putin, look, you're trying to be nice

[161:59]to me, but you're dishonest.

[162:04]Trump said, if you're not serious about peace, then we're going to stop trying.

[162:10]And that was a lie.

[162:12]Trump did not follow through on that at all.

[162:15]Trump said, look, if you don't come to the table and negotiate, we're finished.

[162:20]That was in April.

[162:21]And he lied.

[162:24]So Putin does not take Trump seriously at all.

[162:29]Trump comes out in the middle of July and says, Putin, you have 50 days to make a deal.

[162:34]I'm getting really sick of your stuff.

[162:37]And Putin says, I don't care.

[162:39]And Trump comes back two weeks ago and says, you thought you had 50 days.

[162:43]I've cut it down to 10.

[162:46]And Putin said, I don't care.

[162:49]So then Trump comes to Putin and says, okay, all right, you win.

[162:53]Let's have a meeting.

[162:54]Let's talk about it.

[162:59]Now, Trump sits with Putin and they make progress.

[163:09]they get to all the press conference and Trump gets to have the photo op.

[163:13]He gets to bolster his appeal that he should get a Nobel Prize.

[163:20]He gets a domestic victory.

[163:22]He gets to appear to be a peacemaker.

[163:24]There's this appearance that progress is being made.

[163:36]and say, ah, gee, yeah, well, we really got to keep working on this.

[163:40]Let's schedule another meeting.

[163:41]I don't know, three weeks from now.

[163:43]Let's keep working on this.

[163:45]Let's keep in touch.

[163:46]And in the meantime, the Russians are going to keep taking territory.

[163:50]Ukrainians are evacuating towns that Russia is about to seize in the coming weeks.

[163:59]This is what's going on now to get into the details about the actual positions of both sides

[164:05]What Putin said at the summit today was very interesting for two reasons. We'll talk about two specific things that he said

[164:13]So Putin in the first place, this is very basic in

[164:17]Putin's statement. He said

[164:19]That in order for the war to end

[164:23]We must address the root causes of the conflict, root causes.

[164:28]This is something that he has said over and over for the past three years.

[164:32]What is that a reference to?

[164:35]When Putin says that in order to achieve a peace, we must first address the root causes

[164:40]before a truce, before a ceasefire, before a comprehensive peace.

[164:51]Which is that the reason the war started is because of NATO expansion and really it goes

[164:58]even deeper than that because to Moscow, to Russia, the root cause of the conflict is

[165:05]that Russia as a state has been frozen out of the West ever since the fall of the Soviet

[165:20]There was this initiative on the part of George Bush and Bill Clinton that maybe Russia would

[165:26]be integrated with the United States and Europe.

[165:31]In reality, what has happened is that the United States has pressed its advantage against

[165:36]Russia and continued to treat Russia like an adversary.

[165:40]And perhaps the biggest expression of this is NATO expansion.

[165:49]What you could call the most imminent factor, but it represents something deeper than that,

[165:54]which is that Russia has been ostracized from the West.

[165:59]And what Putin has said for 20 years is that Russia wants to cooperate with Washington.

[166:13]And the reason that Russia feels it's being rebuffed is because Washington is not looking

[166:18]for partners.

[166:20]It's not looking for cooperation.

[166:24]Washington is looking for vassals.

[166:27]Washington is looking for puppet states.

[166:29]It is looking for submission.

[166:31]And so the posture of Russia in the 1990s and the 2000s is that Russia would like to

[166:38]integrate with the United States, would like to cooperate with the United States on a number

[166:44]of issues on Islamic extremism and terrorism, on arms control, on the Arctic, on many different

[166:51]things.

[167:00]unless Russia becomes a full-fledged liberal democracy characterized by openness, intense

[167:07]US influence through NGOs.

[167:11]And Russia says we will not cooperate on those terms.

[167:17]We will not be a vassal. We will not submit to the West and its culture and its norms and things like that.

[167:24]We want to preserve our identity. We want to preserve the unique character of our state and society.

[167:31]We want to maintain our sovereignty.

[167:34]And so if you're looking for a vassal, or rather, if you're looking for a partner, Russia would be a partner, but they won't be a vassal.

[167:41]And so Russia sees itself as in a defensive posture against a very powerful West.

[167:48]Russia is paying a great cost, being sanctioned, being diplomatically isolated, having to fight

[167:56]proxy wars in order to maintain its sovereignty.

[168:01]It is paying a price to maintain its sovereignty and independence.

[168:05]And that comes at the cost of all these different things, financial, sanction, diplomatic isolation

[168:11]and all the rest of it.

[168:13]Now, this is most characterized by NATO's expansion eastward.

[168:17]Russia is excluded from NATO, this extremely powerful defensive alliance led by the United

[168:24]States, which is expanding further and further encroaching on Russia's borders and into its

[168:30]historic heartland.

[168:36]The United States and a security conference, I believe in Bucharest says that one day Ukraine

[168:42]and Georgia are going to join NATO.

[168:45]And this is 17 years ago, Putin says, no, Ukraine and Georgia are not going to join

[168:52]NATO.

[168:53]And that is a part of why Russia launched an incursion into Georgia in 2008.

[168:59]And in 2014, after the Euro-Maidan, after the color revolution in Ukraine, this is why

[169:06]Russia intervened there when they annexed Crimea and began supporting the secession

[169:11]in the Donbas.

[169:14]When Putin says in the speech today, as he said from the beginning, when he says root

[169:19]causes, this is what he is referring to.

[169:25]Georgia, of Ukraine, of all of it. The root cause, it's not just NATO expansion,

[169:33]although that's the biggest part of it. The root cause is that Russia is being

[169:39]frozen out by the West. And that's related to the second thing that he said.

[169:46]So when Putin says, in order to have a comprehensive peace, we need to address the root causes.

[169:52]First and foremost, he means that in order to have a peace, Ukraine cannot join NATO.

[169:58]That's first and foremost the root cause of why a quarter of a million troops amassed

[170:03]on Ukraine's border and invaded in 2022.

[170:07]It's because the West wanted Ukraine to join NATO.

[170:15]at Munich in 2022. So Putin said, the root caught, why did we have to invade Ukraine?

[170:21]Cause you tried to make them join NATO. And because what's more, NATO was arming Ukraine

[170:28]with drones and lethal aid. And what's more, if Ukraine was going to join NATO or be a

[170:36]proxy state for NATO, it would be used as a launch pad for attacks against Russia.

[170:43]So, in order to address these root causes, what Putin says is, we need Crimea.

[170:49]That is where our warm water port is that hosts our Black Sea fleet.

[170:55]We need a land bridge to Crimea, which is those four territories that we've taken.

[171:01]What's more, Ukraine cannot join NATO.

[171:05]You can't put your weapons in Ukraine.

[171:09]It has to be a neutral buffer state and only then can we have peace because we can't be

[171:16]threatened by NATO on our border.

[171:19]We can't be threatened by a Western proxy state this close to our border.

[171:23]We can't be severed from the Black Sea.

[171:26]We cannot be cut off from international trade and waters in that way.

[171:31]Those are the root causes and what that means is that Putin has not backed away from any

[171:37]So, earlier I said, Putin's demands are consistent.

[171:41]He has had the same demands going back to February 22.

[171:45]He articulated them most recently in June in Istanbul when there was a meeting between

[171:50]a Russian and Ukrainian delegation.

[171:53]And those are the terms.

[171:54]You want to stop the bloodshed, you want peace, you want a comprehensive deal.

[171:59]We have to get to the root causes, and that means Ukraine must abandon its bid for NATO membership.

[172:05]They must demilitarize, change their government, and they got to recognize that we have taken this territory.

[172:12]And Washington has accepted some of this.

[172:15]Washington accepts that Ukraine will not join NATO.

[172:18]Washington accepts that there will be some territorial concessions.

[172:24]Washington accepts that Ukraine will be some sort of a buffer state.

[172:28]But the Europeans and the Ukrainians do not they have a very different position on this. That's the first thing that Putin said

[172:35]The second thing that Putin said is that what he really wants is for a general reset in the

[172:42]Relationship between Russia and the West and that's related to the first part. So when Putin says this is about root causes

[172:50]While those concessions might pertain exclusively to the defensive treaty and the architecture

[172:56]of how they're going to defend Ukraine, the true root cause, as I said, is that the West

[173:03]and Russia are estranged.

[173:05]And the big thing that Putin said in the statement also is that he's looking for an

[173:09]all-encompassing reset, meaning that the West and Russia will not just cooperate on

[173:16]Ukraine, but also on arms control.

[173:19]The new start nuclear treaty is set to expire next year.

[173:23]So Russia wants to renew the commitment to arms control between Russia and the West.

[173:30]He also talked about the Arctic.

[173:33]As the North Arctic sea ice is melting, this Northern sea route has opened up on Russia's

[173:43]And they're going to start using that as a shipping lane very soon.

[173:46]It's going to become a very competitive, highly watched shipping lane as the sea ice melts

[173:52]in the Arctic.

[173:54]And as that happens, this is going to become a domain of military competition, of commercial

[174:13]Russia said, China's an Arctic country. You don't board of the Arctic. That's our domain.

[174:19]We are an Arctic country. That northern sea route, that's ours. That's used for intra-Russia

[174:26]shipping. That's not the domain of China. That's an area where the United States and

[174:31]Russia might cooperate against China. And so these are a couple of other domains where

[174:42]Russia, like many of the middle powers, is looking to hedge and play China off of the

[174:49]United States.

[174:51]Russia has been forced in recent times into the arms of China, because what Russia and

[174:57]China have in common is that they're resisting American hegemony.

[175:06]and impose their own region of influence,

[175:10]their own sphere of influence.

[175:11]And so for that reason,

[175:13]they're in this strategic alliance for now.

[175:16]And China needs the raw materials from Russia,

[175:18]like oil and gas.

[175:20]And Russia needs advanced electronics

[175:22]and military equipment from China.

[175:25]But that will not be the case for long

[175:27]because China and Russia share a large border.

[175:31]China and Russia naturally are competitors.

[175:36]China has its eye on the Russian Far East, on Siberia,

[175:41]on the natural resources there.

[175:43]Russia cannot defend that territory.

[175:45]That's a giant vulnerability for them.

[175:48]And so as we go into the future,

[175:51]Russia and the United States also have this strategic goal

[175:55]in mind that certainly there'll be more cooperation

[175:58]between Moscow and Washington, contra Beijing,

[176:01]as Beijing becomes more powerful.

[176:03]So that's the second thing that Putin said.

[176:05]He doesn't just want to solve Ukraine by dealing with some of those tactical considerations

[176:12]in the Eastern European theater.

[176:14]He's got a much broader perspective, which is a complete reset with the West.

[176:19]He wants to emerge from the diplomatic isolation, thaw relations, and have some level of normalcy

[176:26]with the West.

[176:33]Cooperation on arms control and cooperation in the Arctic.

[176:37]That's what Putin said.

[176:38]That's Putin's statement, which is interesting.

[178:24]What does it mean when Ukraine joins NATO?

[178:26]It means that they will be protected under article five.

[178:30]Article five of NATO says an attack on one is an attack on all.

[178:34]It's a tripwire.

[178:35]It means that if any NATO country is attacked, you're declaring war on the United States.

[178:39]That's what that means.

[178:41]Article five means that if some country attacks Italy, well, you're declaring war on America.

[178:52]And nobody wants to go to war with America.

[178:55]So this is what constitutes NATO membership.

[178:59]So Russia says that Ukraine cannot join NATO.

[183:44]I am not advocating for Putin and

[183:48]At the end of the day Vladimir Putin is not our friend

[183:51]He is the leader. He is the sovereign of another country

[184:02]It is just rhetoric. It is just propaganda. As an American, our interest is that we don't

[184:08]wind up dying in a war for Ukraine. That is the America first position. The America first

[184:20]shares a huge border with Russia. It is historically Russian soil. Russia wants it

[184:28]more than we want it because they're willing to fight and die for it and we

[184:32]don't even want to defend it. We don't even want to spend the money to defend it.

[184:36]We don't have the money to defend it. So the America first position is that we do

[184:46]through some cycle of reciprocal escalation,

[184:50]we do not wind up in a confrontation with Russia.

[184:54]That is the America first position,

[184:56]that no Americans die,

[184:59]that we don't spend any more money.

[185:02]that we don't use any more of our depleted stockpile fighting Russia. That's the America

[185:08]first position. What's more, the number one adversary of the United States is going to

[185:15]be China. Now look, I am not an anti-China shill. I don't think that China is our enemy.

[185:30]China is the rising power. China is the rising hegemon. And specifically, China is becoming a regional hegemon in Asia.

[185:41]And Asia is rapidly becoming strategically the most important region in the world. Why?

[185:48]Because that is where all the ships are being made.

[185:52]Nearly all of the world's ships, commercial and military vessels are made in China, South

[186:00]Korea and Japan.

[186:02]That is where all of the chips are being made.

[186:06]All of the advanced chips are being made in Taiwan.

[186:10]All of the electronics that use chips are being packaged in Malaysia, in Vietnam.

[186:21]All the strategic industries are in the Indo-Pacific region.

[186:26]That's where the population has shifted.

[186:28]That's where the advanced industry and manufacturing has shifted.

[186:33]That is becoming, and by the way,

[186:34]also that's where all the raw materials increasingly

[186:37]are coming from.

[186:39]The rare earths, the critical minerals,

[186:42]China has a lot of them.

[186:43]They've got a monopoly on a lot of them.

[186:53]Let Russia have Ukraine.

[186:55]Eastern Europe is not a strategically important region.

[186:58]It just isn't.

[187:00]The Middle East, as time goes on, is becoming less strategically important.

[187:05]The most strategically important region is going to be the Indo-Pacific.

[187:10]the Pacific Ocean, the Indian Ocean, the South and East China Sea.

[187:15]This is where the competition is.

[187:17]This is where the industry is.

[187:19]Now, here's the point.

[187:21]If China is the rising power,

[187:24]there are the rising commercial power, maritime power,

[187:27]there are the rising naval and military power,

[187:30]the rising artificial intelligence power.

[187:33]an electric superpower. They're becoming a superpower in almost everything. And maybe

[187:38]their growth is overstated, but by far and away, they're gonna be the biggest competitor.

[187:53]In terms of our strategic alliances, our alliance with India, Japan, South Korea, Australia,

[187:59]the Quad, this is becoming a far more important strategic relationship than with the Europeans.

[188:06]The United Kingdom and France, these are declining powers.

[188:09]Germany will no longer be an industrial power.

[188:13]The future is in Asia.

[188:23]want to die in a war with Russia.

[188:25]We don't want to spend our money in a war with Russia.

[188:28]Ukraine just isn't that strategically important at the end of the day.

[188:44]as a missed opportunity and say that for the future of American power, we should look to

[188:50]Russia as a potential ally.

[189:07]Russia could certainly be a partner on some issues

[189:11]where there's room to cooperate against China.

[189:14]And to me, from a completely realist

[189:17]and sensible foreign policy perspective,

[189:20]that is what is putting America first.

[189:22]And a lot of people like to say,

[189:24]well, you know, we want China to win.

[189:26]I used to think this and politically

[189:30]there's a case to be made there.

[189:32]We could talk about that.

[189:44]to make stuff.

[190:09]you're gonna need to contain China.

[190:14]that we can cooperate with China.

[190:18]or there has to be a cold war with China,

[190:21]but it is true that if we don't wanna be a declining power

[190:24]like in Europe where there's no growth

[190:26]and we're not competitive

[190:28]and we're not able to defend our interests,

[190:36]the numbers, strictly speaking. There's a case to be made that politically, if China were to displace us,

[191:07]not something we should necessarily be rooting for

[191:10]because if China displaces America

[191:19]where people say, we want Russia

[194:01]Obama and Israel.

[194:21]this on a future show, but like that was such a big shift for me.

[194:24]Elon Musk bought Twitter in October of 2022, October, November 22.

[194:36]because the moment that happened,

[194:38]it just demonstrated the change was possible.

[194:42]He bought Twitter and he just started reinstating everybody.

[194:46]And that seemed like something that,

[194:49]it felt like we were living in a dystopia.

[194:51]It felt like when you got banned on Twitter,

[194:54]you might as well be dead.

[194:55]Cause it's like, you would have a better chance

[194:57]at coming back to life from death

[195:00]than getting back on Twitter.

[195:02]Twitter would take away your account and you're just fucked. No one's getting reinstated. There's no process. There's no forgiveness.

[195:30]You can't have an Uber.
